context: CrisPRO BrM Framework — manuscript brm_graveyard_perspective_v2.md, Section 6.1
description: Structured literature traces for the three anchor citations used in Section 6.1 (The Antigen Escape Problem: Why CAR-T Faces the Same Wall). These citations ground the claim that antigen-targeted CNS immunotherapies face a structural vulnerability profile — antigen escape plus trafficking constraints — that creates a theoretical advantage for SL dependency-based strategies.
ruo_notice: Research Use Only. Not cleared for clinical decision-making.

citations.title: Tumor Antigen Escape from CAR T-cell Therapy
citations.claim_anchored: Antigen loss or downregulation is the primary resistance mechanism in CAR-T therapy and is likely an even greater barrier in solid tumors because of higher antigen heterogeneity.
citations.exact_quote: Emerging data from chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell trials in B-cell malignancies demonstrate that a common mechanism of resistance to this novel class of therapeutics is the emergence of tumors with loss or downregulation of the target antigen. Antigen loss or antigen-low escape is likely to emerge as an even greater barrier to success in solid tumors, which manifest greater heterogeneity in target antigen expression.
citations.manuscript_use: Establishes antigen escape as the structural resistance class shared by CAR-T and vaccines. Grounds the claim that SL-based targeting is less exposed to this failure mode.
citations.notes: Canonical reference for CAR-T antigen escape. Cited in Section 6.1 as [1] in the session literature scout numbering.

citations.authors: O'Rourke DM, Nasrallah MP, Desai A, et al.
citations.title: A single dose of peripherally infused EGFRvIII-directed CAR T cells mediates antigen loss and induces adaptive resistance in patients with recurrent glioblastoma
citations.claim_anchored: In recurrent GBM, EGFRvIII-directed CAR-T cells trafficked to active tumor regions but antigen decrease was observed in post-treatment specimens alongside adaptive resistance.
citations.exact_quote: We found that manufacturing and infusion of CAR-modified T cell (CART)-EGFRvIII cells are feasible and safe... antigen decrease in 5 of 7 evaluable patients, alongside adaptive resistance.
citations.manuscript_use: Direct clinical evidence that EGFRvIII antigen loss occurs under CAR-T pressure in GBM — the cellular therapy parallel to the ACT IV vaccine failure. Confirms the escape mechanism is antigen-targeting-specific, not modality-specific.
citations.notes: First-in-human EGFRvIII CAR-T study. 10 recurrent GBM patients. Cited in Section 6.1 as [50] in the session literature scout numbering.

citations.authors: Li H, Harrison EB, Li H, et al.
citations.title: Targeting brain lesions of non-small cell lung cancer by enhancing CCL2-mediated CAR-T cell migration
citations.claim_anchored: For NSCLC brain metastasis, B7-H3 CAR-T cells required engineering with CCR2b to exploit the CCL2 gradient and improve BBB passage — BBB trafficking is a real engineering barrier, not a solved problem.
citations.exact_quote: CAR T cells targeting B7-H3 (B7-H3.CAR) exhibit antitumor activity in vitro... [CCR2b engineering] significantly improved migration through the BBB and intracranial antitumor activity.
citations.manuscript_use: Establishes that CAR-T trafficking to NSCLC brain lesions requires active engineering intervention. Grounds the claim that CNS delivery is a second structural constraint on top of antigen escape.
citations.notes: NSCLC BrM-specific CAR-T study. Cited in Section 6.1 as [6] in the session literature scout numbering.

citations.authors: Mishra A, Maiti R, Mohan P, et al.
citations.title: Antigen loss following CAR-T cell therapy: Mechanisms, implications, and potential solutions
citations.claim_anchored: Antigen loss or downregulation accounts for 30-70% of relapses in patients with recurrent disease after CAR-T.
citations.manuscript_use: Quantifies the prevalence of antigen escape across CAR-T trials. Cited in Section 6.1 as [2] in the session literature scout numbering.
citations.pmid_status: UNVERIFIED — DOI 10.1111/ejh.14101 not found in PubMed search. Citation may be hallucinated by prior agent. PUBLICATION-BLOCKING: claim (30-70% antigen loss relapse rate) must be re-anchored to a verified source before submission.

citations.authors: Tang M, Xu M, Wang J, et al.
citations.title: Brain Metastasis from EGFR-Mutated Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Secretion of IL11 from Astrocytes Up-Regulates PDL1 and Promotes Immune Escape
citations.claim_anchored: In EGFR-mutant NSCLC BrM, reactive astrocytes secrete IL-11 which upregulates PD-L1 and promotes immune escape — a CNS-specific immunosuppressive mechanism.
citations.manuscript_use: Establishes brain-specific immunosuppressive mechanism as a third structural constraint on antigen-targeted approaches in the CNS. Cited in Section 6.1 as [70] in the session literature scout numbering.
citations.pmid_status: UNRESOLVED — DOI confirmed (10.1002/advs.202306348), paper verified on PubMed but PMID blocked by reCAPTCHA. Manual lookup required.

citations.authors: Weller M, Butowski N, Tran D, et al.
citations.title: Rindopepimut with temozolomide for patients with newly diagnosed, EGFRvIII-expressing glioblastoma (ACT IV)
citations.claim_anchored: ACT IV Phase III trial: HR 1.01, p=0.93. EGFRvIII expression lost in recurrent tumors from vaccinated patients — immunoediting confirmed as mechanism of failure.
citations.manuscript_use: The definitive proof-of-concept that antigen-targeted immunotherapy fails through immunoediting in brain tumors. Central to Section 3.4 and the paradigm shift argument in Section 6.

provenance.scout_method: LiteratureSearch via Consensus API + targeted PubMed verification
